sentence: tam fortis erat ut omnēs eum admīrārentur.
display: tam fortis erat ut omnēs eum admīrārentur.
translation: He was so brave that everyone admired him.
options: result clause
purpose clause
temporal clause
indirect statement
explanation: tam (so) in the main clause + ut + subjunctive (admīrārentur) signals a result clause. The key diagnostic is the intensifying word tam before the adjective. Ask: 'what happened as a result of his bravery?' → everyone admired him. Purpose would answer 'why?' not 'what happened?'.

translation: He hurried so much that he arrived soon.
options: result clause
indirect command
purpose clause
causal clause
explanation: ita (so/in such a way) in the main clause + ut + subjunctive (adveniret) signals a result clause. ita modifies the verb (he hurried so much) rather than an adjective. The result clause describes the consequence — arriving soon — not the aim.

sentence: adeo stultus erat ut omnia crēderet.
display: adeo stultus erat ut omnia crēderet.
translation: He was so stupid that he believed everything.
options: result clause
purpose clause
indirect statement
fearing clause
explanation: adeō (so very/to such a degree) before stultus + ut + subjunctive (crēderet) is the result clause pattern. adeō is a strong intensifier. The consequence — believing everything — follows from the degree of stupidity described in the main clause.

sentence: tanta tempestās erat ut nāvēs mergerentur.
display: tanta tempestās erat ut nāvēs mergerentur.
translation: The storm was so great that the ships were sunk.
options: result clause
purpose clause
temporal clause
indirect command
explanation: tantus/tanta/tantum (so great) before the noun + ut + subjunctive (mergerentur) is the result clause pattern using a size/degree adjective. mergerentur is imperfect subjunctive passive (were being sunk). The ships' sinking is the consequence of the storm's greatness.

sentence: tot mīlitēs aderant ut hostēs fugerent.
display: tot mīlitēs aderant ut hostēs fugerent.
translation: So many soldiers were present that the enemy fled.
options: result clause
purpose clause
causal clause
indirect statement
explanation: tot (so many) before the noun mīlitēs + ut + subjunctive (fugerent) signals a result clause. tot is the numeral intensifier (alongside tam, ita, adeō, tantus). The flight of the enemy is the consequence of having so many soldiers present.

sentence: tam sapienter locūtus est ut omnēs tacerent.
display: tam sapienter locūtus est ut omnēs tacerent.
options: He spoke so wisely that everyone fell silent.
He spoke wisely in order that everyone would fall silent.
He spoke wisely because everyone had fallen silent.
He spoke so wisely that he knew everyone was silent.
correct: He spoke so wisely that everyone fell silent.
explanation: tam (so) before the adverb sapienter + ut + subjunctive (tacerent) is a result clause. Note that tam can intensify adverbs as well as adjectives. The result clause (everyone fell silent) is the consequence of speaking so wisely — it happened as a result, not as the aim.

sentence: adeō mīrātī sumus ut verbum dīcere nōn possemus.
display: adeō mīrātī sumus ut verbum dīcere nōn possemus.
options: We were so amazed that we could not say a word.
We were amazed in order that we could say a word.
We were so amazed that we said not a word.
We were amazed because we could not say a word.
correct: We were so amazed that we could not say a word.
explanation: adeō (so very) + mīrātī sumus + ut + subjunctive (possemus) is a result clause. possemus is imperfect subjunctive of possum (to be able). The inability to speak is the consequence of the degree of amazement — it resulted from being so amazed.

sentence: ita clāmāvit ut omnēs cīvēs perterritī sint.
display: ita clāmāvit ut omnēs cīvēs perterritī sint.
options: He shouted so loudly that all the citizens were terrified.
He shouted so that all the citizens would be terrified.
He shouted although all the citizens were terrified.
He shouted because all the citizens had been terrified.
correct: He shouted so loudly that all the citizens were terrified.
explanation: ita (in such a way/so much) + clāmāvit + ut + perfect subjunctive passive (perterritī sint) is a result clause. The perfect subjunctive in result clauses can indicate a vivid or realised result. The terror is the consequence of the shouting's intensity.

sentence: tantum vīnum bibit ut sub mēnsā caderet.
display: tantum vīnum bibit ut sub mēnsā caderet.
options: He drank so much wine that he fell under the table.
He drank wine in order to fall under the table.
He drank such good wine that it fell under the table.
He feared that the wine would make him fall under the table.
correct: He drank so much wine that he fell under the table.
explanation: tantum (so much, accusative neuter of tantus) modifying vīnum + ut + subjunctive (caderet) is a result clause. The falling is the consequence of drinking so much wine. Purpose would require 'in order to fall' as the aim — here falling is clearly a result, not an intention.

sentence: tam diu pugnāvērunt ut dēfessī essent.
display: tam diu pugnāvērunt ut dēfessī essent.
options: They fought for so long that they were exhausted.
They fought for a long time in order to become exhausted.
They fought for a long time although they were exhausted.
They knew they had fought so long that they were exhausted.
correct: They fought for so long that they were exhausted.
explanation: tam diū (for so long — tam intensifying the temporal adverb diū) + ut + subjunctive (essent) is a result clause. The exhaustion is the consequence of fighting for so long. The intensifier tam attached to a temporal adverb is a variant of the standard pattern.
